texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most of the energy in the world comes from fossil fuels, such as coal, oil and gas. However, the combustion of fossil fuels inevitably results in the emission of air pollutants and a huge release of carbondioxide (CO2). Since CO2 is a major greenhouse gas, it should be removed from industrial flue gas streams. Many methods exist to remove CO2 by absorption into aqueous solution of alkanolamines using conventional equipment including packed columns, bubble columns, and spray columns. The use of aqueous alkanolamines allows regeneration of the liquid absorbents by simple heating. Therefore, a typical process for CO2 capture consists of two major units, absorption and desorption. Desorption is commonly carried out by using conventional columns that have operational problems suchasflooding, channeling, and entrainment. Usually, the stripper unit operates at slightly above normal pressure and high temperature. A few studies reported CO2 desorption based on conventional columns [1,2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactor based CO2 stripping experiments were conducted using loaded CO2 monoethanolamine(MEA)solutionasanabsorbentwhileN2 gaswasusedasastrippinggas.Amembrane module made from stainless steel was developed to test a variety of membrane cartridges. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) hollow fiber membranes were used in this work to test the desorption performance and membrane stability in gas stripping membrane contactor. The effect of the operating variables investigated included gas and liquid velocities, MEA concentration, and temperature. The experimental results showed that the stripping gas velocity had a minor effect on the CO2 desorption flux and mass transfer coefficients while the increase in the liquid velocity, operating temperature, and solution concentration could enhance CO2 desorption flux in the gas stripping membrane contactor. However, the CO2 desorption flux started to decrease at 5.0 kmol m -3 MEAsolution. A high membrane porosity showed a superior desorption performance, but the long term performance deteriorated due to the membrane wetting.
texts.text: Membrane contactor based CO2 stripping experiments were conducted using loaded CO2 monoethanolamine(MEA)solutionasanabsorbentwhileN2 gaswasusedasastrippinggas.Amembrane module made from stainless steel was developed to test a variety of membrane cartridges. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) hollow fiber membranes were used in this work to test the desorption performance and membrane stability in gas stripping membrane contactor. The effect of the operating variables investigated included gas and liquid velocities, MEA concentration, and temperature. The experimental results showed that the stripping gas velocity had a minor effect on the CO2 desorption flux and mass transfer coefficients while the increase in the liquid velocity, operating temperature, and solution concentration could enhance CO2 desorption flux in the gas stripping membrane contactor. However, the CO2 desorption flux started to decrease at 5.0 kmol m -3 MEAsolution. A high membrane porosity showed a superior desorption performance, but the long term performance deteriorated due to the membrane wetting.

texts.orig: tially applied for desorption or regeneration of liquid absorbents. Kumazawa [8] investigated CO2 desorption from CO2 loaded 2amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) solution using the membrane contacting process. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) hollow fiber membranes were used in the experiments. It was found that the desorption process was controlled by diffusion and chemical reaction in the liquid film. The results showed that the overall mass transfer coefficient increased with an increase in AMP solution concentration and CO2 loading in the solution. Recently, Koonaphapdeelert et al. [9] studied CO2 desorption from MEA solutions in ceramic hollow fiber membrane contactors. They found that the height of the transfer unit (HTU) of the contactors was as low as 15cm. The mass transfer resistance in the liquid phase was a dominant resistance in the stripping membrane contactors, the samebehavior as found in the gas absorption membrane contactor. Ceramic membranes show high chemical and thermal stabilities which are suitable to use at high temperature applications. However, ceramic membranes are extremely expensive because the fabrication method involves a high temperature sintering treatment.

texts.orig: In this work, membrane contactor based regeneration unit was developed to study CO2 stripping from monoethanolamine (MEA). Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) hollow fiber membranes were used in the experiments because they have shown superior chemical resistance and thermal stability than other polymeric membranes. These properties make PTFE membranes suitable for this application. The operating conditions that affect system performance such as gas and liquid velocities, MEA concentration and rich solution temperature were investigated to study the mass transfer process

texts.orig: Kumazawa [8] and Koonaphapdeelert et al. [9] who found that the mass transfer in gas stripping membrane contactors was mainly controlled by the liquid film mass transfer coefficient. Khaisri et al. [17] reported that the gas phase mass transfer resistance in the membrane contacting process was roughly 5% of the overall mass transfer coefficient. It can be concluded that the liquid mass transfer resistance is the controlling mass transfer resistance in desorption processes using membrane contactors. This behavior was also found in the gas absorption membrane contactor as well as the gas stripping process in a packed column [3,4,18].

texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows that the liquid phase mass transfer resistance accounted for roughly 90% of the overall mass transfer resistance. An increase in the liquid velocity resulted in an increase in the CO2 desorption flux at any given operating temperature as seen in Fig. 5. The results confirmed the previous discussion that the liquid phase mass transfer resistance is the controlling resistance in the system. An increase in the liquid velocity leads to a decrease in the liquidfilm mass transfer resistance. Thus, the CO2 desorption flux, as well as the overall mass transfer coefficient increase with an increase in liquid velocity. The results corresponded to many gas absorption studies in membrane contactors [3,4,11,19].

texts.orig: The effect of rich solution temperature is also shown in Fig. 5. It is obvious that the CO2 desorption flux increased with an increase in the solution temperature. The temperature directly affects the CO2 equilibrium partial pressure, chemical reaction equilibrium constant, and diffusion coefficient. The equilibrium partial pressure of CO2 increases exponentially with temperature. This factor increases by the factors of 5-8 by increasing temperature 10 ◦ C[18]. Therefore, the chemical reaction equilibrium constant decreases due to the effect of equilibrium partial pressure of CO2 . As a result, an increase in operating temperature leads to an increase in the driving force for stripping of CO2 from the MEA solution.

The viscosityofMEAsolutionat100 ◦ Cwitha0.45CO2 loadingincreases by roughly 65% and 200% by increasing concentration from 3.0 to 5.0 and 7.0 kmolm -3 , respectively [20]. The diffusion coefficient of carbamate in MEA solution is also important. The carbamate diffusivity in MEA solution was assumed to be equal to the MEA diffusivity in MEA solution [1]. Hayduk and Laudie [21] reported that thediffusivity decreases with an increase in the viscosity. For example, the diffusivity of carbamate in MEA solution at 25 ◦ C decreases about 48% and 138% by increasing the concentration from 3.0 to 5.0 and 7.0 kmol m -3 , respectively. These factors play an important role in the overall mass transfer coefficient in both CO2 absorption and desorption as reported by some researchers [6,18]. Operation at high solution concentration can also increase a corrosion rate in steel vessel and steel piping increase. This factor needs to be considered and minimized in terms of mass transfer rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 8 shows the effect of membrane porosity on the CO2 desorption performance. It is obvious that membrane porosity plays an important role in membrane contactor systems. The CO2 desorption flux of the module C, which has a higher membrane porosity than the module A, showed a superior performance at any given liquid velocity. Module C had a higher CO2 desorption flux than module A, roughly 150-390%. This can be explained by the influence of membrane porosity on the gas transport through the membrane. A porous membrane with a low membrane porosity has poor gas transport properties. This was also confirmed by Gryta [22] who found that gas permeability increased with an increase the membrane porosity. An increase in the membrane porosity by 1% resulted in an increase in the gas permeability by roughly 58%. It was also shown that a high air flux was achieved with a high membrane porosity. When the membrane porosity increases, therearemoreavailablemembraneporesforCO2 todiffusethrough the membrane. Moreover, it can be described based on liquid film boundary layer. Matsumoto et al. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A membrane contactor based regeneration unit was developed to strip CO2 gas from CO2 loaded monoethanolamine solution. PTFE hollow fiber membranes were used to test the desorption performance. The experimental results showed that the CO2 desorption fluxincreasedwithanincreaseintheliquidvelocity,operatingtemperature, and MEA concentration. However, increasing the MEA concentration too high resulted in the decrease of the overall mass transfer coefficient due to the effect of viscosity. The maximum MEA concentration that gave the highest CO2 desorption performance in this work was 5kmolm -3 . The gas phase mass transfer resistance in gas stripping membranes was found to have a minor effect on the CO2 desorption flux as generally found in a gas absorption membrane. This work also showed that the desorption rate increased by a factor of two when the available membrane surface was double. It indicated that the gas stripping membrane contactor can be linearly scaled-up. Membrane porosity affected the CO2 desorption flux as well as the membrane wetting. The experimental results also showed that high membrane porosity resulted in a high desorption performance, but the long term performance dropped due to the membrane wetting.
texts.text: A membrane contactor based regeneration unit was developed to strip CO2 gas from CO2 loaded monoethanolamine solution. PTFE hollow fiber membranes were used to test the desorption performance. The experimental results showed that the CO2 desorption fluxincreasedwithanincreaseintheliquidvelocity,operatingtemperature, and MEA concentration. However, increasing the MEA concentration too high resulted in the decrease of the overall mass transfer coefficient due to the effect of viscosity. The maximum MEA concentration that gave the highest CO2 desorption performance in this work was 5kmolm -3 . The gas phase mass transfer resistance in gas stripping membranes was found to have a minor effect on the CO2 desorption flux as generally found in a gas absorption membrane. This work also showed that the desorption rate increased by a factor of two when the available membrane surface was double. It indicated that the gas stripping membrane contactor can be linearly scaled-up. Membrane porosity affected the CO2 desorption flux as well as the membrane wetting. The experimental results also showed that high membrane porosity resulted in a high desorption performance, but the long term performance dropped due to the membrane wetting.
